afd_data.text:
.AVIATION...
(18Z TAFS)
Issued at 1010 AM PDT Sun Mar 29 2026

Currently VFR at all terminals with high clouds streaming in and
stratus remaining confined to the Big Sur Coast. Moderate to high
confidence on VFR prevailing through the TAF period. Tonight another
southerly surge may try again and conditions will cool and moisten
with an approaching cold front, but confidence is low that sub-VFR
conditions will develop at the terminals as a result. Diurnal winds
will prevail. Haze will reduce slant range visibilities.

Vicinity of SFO...Currently VFR with light northeasterly flow. High
confidence in VFR through the TAF period. Winds will back through
the TAF period, maintaining diurnal patterns.

SFO Bridge Approach...Similar to SFO.

Monterey Bay Terminals...Currently VFR and calm at MRY and VFR with
drainage flow at SNS. Moderate confidence on VFR prevailing through
the TAF period. A southerly surge may try again tonight, but
confidence is low that it`ll impact the terminals. Even if the
terminals aren`t directly impacted, low ceilings/visibilities should
be expected within the area, especially along the Big Sur Coast.
Diurnal winds will prevail.

.AVIATION /18Z TAFS THROUGH 18Z MONDAY/...

VFR conditions currently at all sites, which will stay the course
through the period. An incoming system will continue to drop
ceilings to between 6-10kft this afternoon, with breezy wind gusts
of 20-30kts. Winds will subside this evening as ceilings lift and
break related to high pressure moving in behind the departing
system. 75

prompts.system:
You are an experienced CFII/charter pilot briefing another pilot.
Be direct, practical, and evidence-based. Write in plain pilot language.

CORE RULES

1) Evidence discipline
Only describe a hazard as affecting the flight if it overlaps the route, time window, and altitude band.
Do not overstate localized or uncertain conditions.
Use:
- likely = strong direct support
- possible / conditional = limited or localized support
- low confidence = weak or missing support

2) Icing rule
Icing requires both subfreezing temperatures and moisture/cloud evidence.
Do not infer icing from temperature alone.
If icing probability is low or moisture is absent, say icing is not a significant factor.
If no SLD signal exists, say so explicitly.

3) Terrain rule
Distinguish large-scale weather from terrain-driven effects.
If terrain effects are expected, explain the operational impact briefly:
departure, en route crossing, arrival.
Do not describe terrain effects as route-wide unless guidance supports it.

4) Decision framework
At the beginning, state:
- what kind of day this is
- the primary operational risk
- GO / MARGINAL GO / NO-GO
- the single most important trigger that would change the decision

For the primary risk, give 1-3 practical decision triggers, with thresholds where possible.

5) AFD rule
Use AFD only as supporting context.
It may:
- increase or decrease confidence
- highlight forecaster concern or uncertainty
It may not:
- introduce hazards unsupported by forecast data
- override chart evidence

6) Forecast maturity
If more than 48 hours out, treat confidence as limited.
24-48 hours out: moderate confidence unless data strongly agrees.
Within 24 hours: be as decisive as the evidence allows.

INPUT PRIORITY
Use, in order:
1. TAFs / surface forecast
2. winds aloft
3. icing / turbulence / cloud / QPF products
4. G-AIRMET / SIGMET / SigWx
5. AFD for confidence adjustment

TCF +04hr (valid Sun 22Z) - TCF +08hr (valid Mon 02Z) - ETCF +10hr (valid Mon 04Z) - ETCF +14hr (valid Mon 08Z) - ETCF +16hr (valid Mon 10Z) - SIGMET Current (valid Sun 18Z) - SigWx Low +00hr (valid Sun 00Z) - SigWx Low +12hr (valid Mon 00Z) - SigWx Low +18hr (valid Mon 12Z) THEN a blank line, then the pilot briefing below. --- REQUIRED SECTIONS (use these exact h2 headings):

Executive Summary

One short paragraph with exactly: a) What kind of day this is (e.g., "This is a VMC wind/terrain day, not a weather system problem.") b) Primary Risk: one sentence c) GO / MARGINAL GO / NO-GO d) The single most important trigger to monitor Do not expand beyond these four elements. A pilot should read only this and know the call.

The Day Before — What to Watch

Short practical re-check list for the evening before. What to look for and what would change the go/no-go.

Leaving KSQL — Departure at 2026-03-30 14:00Z

Describe departure ceiling/visibility/wind. State whether this is: - VMC departure - brief layer penetration - likely departure IMC (only if TAFs show BKN/OVC at or below climb altitudes) Use pilot language, not METAR codes.

The Ride at 12,000 ft

WINDS ALOFT: If winds/temps data is provided, decode and present actual winds at cruise altitude for stations along the route. Calculate headwind/tailwind/crosswind components. RIDE QUALITY: Classify using standard terms only: smooth / light chop / light turbulence / moderate turbulence / severe turbulence Wind-over-terrain reference: - <20 kt: minimal terrain effects - 20-30 kt: light to moderate terrain turbulence possible - 30-40 kt: mountain wave likely, moderate turbulence possible - >40 kt: moderate or greater turbulence likely ICING: Include a vertical icing table: Altitude | Icing Prob | Notes State: freezing level, worst icing band, SLD risk (or explicitly no SLD signal). ALTITUDE STRATEGY: State explicitly: a) best altitude band and why b) worst altitude band and why c) escape option if conditions worsen: climb, descend, divert, or turn around HAZARD TABLE: Hazard | Risk | Leg | What to Expect Incorporate icing (FIP), turbulence (GTG), G-AIRMET, SIGMET, SigWx, and QPF findings.

Getting Into KBDN

Describe expected arrival conditions. Estimate block time. Include: runway wind estimate with uncertainty, practical alternates. Distinguish: non-event VMC arrival / terrain-localized issue / broader weather issue. For terrain airports: do not assume winds aloft = runway winds. State uncertainty explicitly.

Fly or No

Verdict first: GO / MARGINAL GO / NO-GO. Hard stops, then watch items. If recommending IFR, state why: IMC, terrain complexity, or workload/safety margin.

What changes this call

- 2-4 bullets max - at least one quantitative trigger (winds > X kt, ceilings < X ft)

If You Go — Do This

Concrete action items only: altitude, alternates, fuel, what to check morning-of. If departure timing materially changes risk (convection, frontal passage, diurnal winds, marine layer), state best and worst windows with a one-sentence reason each. If timing is not a factor, omit.

Confidence

State High / Medium / Low and what forecast elements could still change the call. Do not assign High if terrain-driven effects dominate or critical data is missing. FACTUAL ACCURACY: Do not guess airport characteristics (towered/non-towered, airspace class, runway count, elevation) unless you are certain. If unsure, omit rather than state incorrectly. Use only the airport names and data provided in the FLIGHT header. Respond with HTML using: h2, h3, h4, p, ul, ol, li, strong, em, blockquote, table, thead, tbody, tr, th, td, code, hr. No html/head/body/style/script tags.

briefing_html:
Executive Summary

This is a post-frontal clearing day at the departure end with a moderately active Pacific system arriving over the Oregon Cascades right around your planned arrival window. Primary risk: deteriorating ceilings and icing at cruise and in the Cascade descent into KBDN, coinciding with your ETA. The TAF for KBDN shows ceilings dropping from broken 10,000 ft before 08Z to broken 3,500 ft and overcast 7,000 ft by 08Z, then overcast 5,000 ft by 12Z — you land at ~16Z when the TAF calls OVC100, which looks manageable on paper, but the frontal timing has uncertainty. MARGINAL GO — flyable for an IFR-equipped and current crew with a solid alternate plan, not a comfortable VFR day. Single most important trigger: KBDN TAF amendment or PIREP showing ceilings below 2,500 ft and/or icing reports in the Cascades at 12,000 ft within two hours of departure.

The Day Before — What to Watch

  • Evening TAF update for KBDN (~00Z Monday): The FM300800 group is the most critical — if that BKN035 OVC070 group verifies or tightens further, your arrival window compresses significantly. Look for any amendment pushing that deterioration earlier than 08Z.
  • Frontal timing: The 18Z Mon surface prog shows the cold front clearing Oregon by 12Z Monday. If the front slows by 3–4 hours, your arrival catches it. Check the 06Z Monday surface analysis when it posts.
  • Icing AIRMETs overnight: The G-AIRMET Icing +12hr shows icing from the freezing level to FL180/FL210 along the Pacific Northwest corridor. Confirm whether that icing band has retreated north of your route by morning-of.
  • Pireps over the Cascades: Any pirep showing moderate icing or ceilings below 3,000 ft near Redmond/Bend (RDM) changes your go/no-go to NO-GO without a timing adjustment.
  • Winds: RDM 12,000 ft forecast is 255/50 kt at the valid time. If anything, that may be the strongest wind zone of the flight — check for any mountain wave SIGMETs or pireps over the Oregon Cascades.

Leaving KSQL — Departure at 2026-03-30 14:00Z

Departure looks clean. The SFO TAF (nearest to KSQL at 9 nm) calls broken 25,000 ft or higher through the period with winds 280/09 kt shifting lighter overnight. By the 07Z FM group it's 270/03 kt, still VFR. The MTR AFD confirms high confidence VFR at the Bay Area terminals through the TAF period with only a low-confidence southerly surge possible overnight — well after your 14Z departure.

This is a VMC departure. Expect a straightforward climb through clear or high-thin cirrus, with no layer penetration required. Haze reducing slant range vis is noted in the AFD — don't expect stellar in-cockpit forward visibility below 8,000 ft over the Bay, but this is a non-issue operationally. Surface winds from the west-northwest around 8–10 kt favor Runway 30 at KSQL.

The Ride at 12,000 ft

Winds Aloft

Data valid 30/06Z, for use 03–12Z. Your flight departs 14Z — these are slightly early but the best available. Interpolating for en route use:

Station6,000 ft9,000 ft12,000 ftTemp @ 12K
SFO (departure)230/05231/10241/15-1°C
SAC (mid-route)301/15261/11251/17-1°C
RDM (arrival)252/22254/40255/50-6°C

The route runs roughly south-to-north. At cruise (12,000 ft), winds at SFO/SAC are west-southwest 15–17 kt — expect a minor left crosswind component, essentially neutral to slightly beneficial. Over the Oregon Cascades (RDM), winds jump to 255/50 kt at 12,000 ft. That's a 10–15 kt headwind component on a northbound track, but more importantly it's a 50-kt westerly crossing the Cascade crest — mountain wave territory.

Ride Quality

Expect smooth to light chop over California at 12,000 ft. Over the Cascades, with 50 kt westerlies crossing the terrain, moderate turbulence is likely, particularly in the 10,000–14,000 ft band on the lee (east) side. The GTG turbulence chart at +15hr shows orange (moderate) values over central Oregon at 12,000 ft, consistent with the mountain wave environment. The G-AIRMET Turb/LLWS +12hr confirms TurbHi 240–420 over the Montana/Idaho region and shows a broad low-level turbulence area developing. At 12,000 ft over the Cascades, light-to-moderate is the floor; moderate is the most likely call.

Icing

Freezing level: At SFO/SAC, temps at 12,000 ft are near -1°C — you're just above freezing at cruise altitude near departure. At RDM, 12,000 ft is -6°C. The G-AIRMET Freezing +12hr shows the freezing level at the surface from roughly the Idaho/Montana border westward, and near 4,000–8,000 ft over northern California/Oregon.

AltitudeIcing Probability (Mon ~09Z)SeverityNotes
6,000 ftLow (terrain mask over Rockies)Trace-lightBelow freezing level over NorCal
9,000 ftLow–Moderate (NW corridor)LightGreen coverage over PNW approach
12,000 ft (cruise)Moderate over Oregon/CascadesLight-ModerateBlue (light-mod) severity; FIP green-yellow
15,000 ftLow–Moderate (same corridor)LightIcing band shallower here
18,000 ftLowTraceAbove main icing band

Worst icing band: 9,000–13,000 ft over the Oregon Cascades, coinciding with cloud layers and the frontal system. The G-AIRMET Icing +12hr shows icing FZL to 18,000 ft over the Pacific Northwest. The FIP at +15hr shows light-to-moderate severity at 12,000 ft with localized moderate patches. SLD risk: Isolated SLD pixels exist at +15hr over the Oregon/Idaho corridor — not widespread, but not zero. Given the mixed precip environment (snow/mix signals on surface progs over Oregon), treat this as a possible SLD environment near the Cascades. If you do not have FIKI or equivalent protection, this band warrants serious consideration.

Altitude Strategy

  • Best band for California: 10,000–12,000 ft — above most terrain, below significant icing, light winds, smooth ride.
  • Best band for the Cascade crossing: 14,000–16,000 ft — above the most problematic icing layer, still in the moderate wind zone but reduced wave turbulence severity aloft, and above the cloud decks near KBDN.
  • Worst band: 9,000–12,000 ft over the Oregon Cascades — highest icing probability, possible SLD, and strongest mountain wave turbulence.
  • Escape option: If icing accumulates in the Cascades and you can't climb above the layer, your primary escape is a turn back or diversion to KRDM/KPSC. Descending below the freezing level (which is near the surface over central Oregon in winter conditions) is not practical here. Climbing to FL170–180 gets you above most of the icing band.

Hazard Table

HazardRiskLegWhat to Expect
Icing at cruise (FIP + G-AIRMET)ModerateOregon CascadesLight-moderate icing 9,000–13,000 ft with frontal clouds; possible SLD — ice protection required
Mountain wave / turbulence (GTG + G-AIRMET)ModerateCascade crossing / KBDN descent50 kt westerlies over terrain; moderate turbulence likely, especially on lee descent. Secure cabin.
KBDN arrival ceilings (TAF)ModerateArrivalOVC050 by 12Z, clearing to OVC100 by 16Z. Ceiling trend depends on frontal timing — marginal IFR possible
Frontal timing uncertaintyModerateEn route / arrivalFront over Oregon at your ETA. 2–3 hour slip in frontal timing = IMC arrival
SIGMET (current)Low / not on route—Active SIGMET is over Baja/Mexico — not a factor for this route
Departure weatherLowKSQL departureVFR, no significant hazards

Getting Into KBDN

KBDN sits at 3,459 ft MSL in the high desert east of the Cascades. Your ETA is approximately 16Z (based on ~2:20 flight time at 12,000 ft with the winds aloft).

The KBDN TAF for your arrival window (16Z):

FM301600 33005KT P6SM OVC100

That's overcast at 10,000 ft — roughly 6,500 ft AGL — with 5 kt from the north-northwest. That's a serviceable VMC arrival if it verifies. However, the 12Z group shows OVC050 (about 1,500 ft AGL), which is marginal VFR to IFR. The question is whether the front clears by 16Z as forecast. The PDT AFD says ceilings will "lift and break related to high pressure moving in behind the departing system" — so clearing is expected, but the exact timing has uncertainty.

Runway wind estimate: FM301600 calls 330/05 kt — light north-northwest, favoring Runway 34. No significant gusts forecast by arrival. However, with 50 kt westerlies at 12,000 ft crossing the Cascades, the terrain-sheltered surface wind at KBDN can be highly variable in the descent. Don't plan for that 5 kt light wind to be the whole story on approach — there may be locally gusty or variable conditions on the PAPI approaches to Runway 16/34 depending on wave activity. Uncertainty: moderate.

Practical alternates:

  • KRDM (Roberts Field, Redmond) — 15 nm north: Commercial airport with ILS, same general weather but slightly better infrastructure. Primary alternate.
  • KPDT (Pendleton) — 100 nm northeast: In the clear high-pressure area behind the front — solid alternate if the whole Oregon Cascade region goes down.
  • KRNO (Reno) — 200 nm southeast: If you can't get into the Oregon high desert at all, Reno is solidly in the clear high-pressure ridge.

File with KRDM as alternate minimum. Brief for an instrument approach at KBDN; the PAPI serves the visual but you want options.

Fly or No

MARGINAL GO — for an IFR-equipped aircraft with ice protection and a current IFR pilot. This is not a VFR cross-country.

Hard stops:

  • No ice protection (FIKI or approved de-ice): NO-GO. The icing environment over the Cascades at cruise altitude is real, with possible SLD. Without protection you have no safe escape.
  • Not IFR current: NO-GO. KBDN arrival ceilings may require an instrument approach.
  • KBDN TAF amended to show ceilings below minimums with no alternate in the clear: NO-GO.

Watch items:

  • Frontal timing — if any morning data shows the front 3+ hours behind schedule, the 16Z arrival window closes.
  • Pireps over the Cascades showing moderate or severe icing — changes this to NO-GO at 12,000 ft without a higher altitude escape route.

What changes this call

  • KBDN TAF amended to OVC030 or lower at your ETA — flip to NO-GO or delay until post-frontal clearing is confirmed.
  • Pireps reporting moderate icing at 12,000–14,000 ft between KSQL and KBDN — route is closed for non-ice-protected aircraft; reconsider altitude or timing even with FIKI.
  • RDM 12,000 ft winds verified above 55 kt — mountain wave turbulence severity likely increases to moderate-severe; consider FL170+ or delay.
  • Morning METARs at KBDN showing OVC below 3,000 ft at 12Z with no trend improvement — front is running late, push departure to 16Z or later.

If You Go — Do This

  1. Aircraft: Confirm IFR current, ice protection is fully operational and tested before departure. File IFR.
  2. Altitude plan: File 12,000 ft for California, request FL140–160 for the Cascade crossing to stay above the worst icing band and above the wave turbulence core. Confirm with ATC — Seattle Center covers this airspace.
  3. Fuel: Plan for KRDM as filed alternate. Add 45 minutes contingency on top of IFR reserves given the frontal timing uncertainty. KBDN has fuel (FBO hours 08–17 local / 16–01Z), but confirm availability for your arrival.
  4. Morning-of (before departure):
    • Pull the latest KBDN TAF — look specifically at whether the FM301200 OVC050 group has amended earlier or later.
    • Check for new pireps over the Oregon Cascades on the SIGMET/CWA page.
    • Confirm no new SIGMETs for icing over the PNW corridor.
    • Pull the 12Z surface analysis when it posts (~14Z) to confirm frontal position.
  5. Departure timing:
    • 14Z as planned is the best window — KSQL is clear, and you arrive at KBDN after the expected frontal passage at 16Z.
    • Pushing to 16Z or later is your safety margin if morning data shows the front is behind schedule — an extra hour gives KBDN time to clear to OVC100 as forecast.
    • Avoid departing after 17Z local (00Z Tuesday) — you'd be arriving in deteriorating post-frontal conditions and in the dark at a non-towered terrain airport.
  6. Seattle Center: For CD when KBDN ATCT is closed, contact Seattle ARTCC at 253-351-3694 as noted in the AFD.
  7. Brief your passenger/crew on the possibility of diverting to KRDM if KBDN is below minimums on arrival. It's 15 minutes away and has a full ILS.

Confidence

Medium confidence. The departure picture is solid — Bay Area VFR is well-supported. The uncertainty is entirely at the Oregon end:

  • Frontal timing over the Oregon Cascades is the key variable — a 2–3 hour error in either direction materially changes the arrival conditions at KBDN.
  • The icing environment over the Cascades at cruise is real and the FIP/G-AIRMET/SLD data agrees on a legitimate icing band, but exact intensity depends on cloud depth and frontal moisture, which is harder to pin down 12–15 hours out.
  • Mountain wave strength at 50 kt could be higher — the GTG at 12,000 ft shows moderate values but wave amplitude is terrain-dependent and hard to forecast precisely.
  • Confidence would rise to high with a favorable morning TAF update for KBDN showing clearing by 14Z and no Cascade icing pireps.
